occurrenceRemarks:
52601635603 Here is a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) among cactus spines (and lichens!) in the garden this morning. I believe this is a juvenile of the species Araneus andrewsi spider with humps on its back and dark colors and bristly legs. I have a prior shot of its web, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2023)

After the driest start of any start of any year that I can recall, the month of December has been better. We got 4.25 inches yesterday and 17.8 inches of rain for the month -- and there's more on the way!

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52601532255 This is a group of beautiful Frosted Marasmius (Marasmius plicatulus or kin) mushrooms poking up through the fallen oak leaves in the woods today. This may actually be a related species of the mushroom because the cap isn't especially velvety or frosted as they sometimes are? The widely-spaced white gills are especially handsome. The stalks are tough and polished, but brittle - like thin glass tubing. If you have pierced ears, you can wear them! (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52600599232 This is a brand new Earthstar ""mushroom"" just coming up in the soggy woods today. I added scare quotes because these fungi are not at all fleshy. It is the Hygroscopic Earthstar (Astraeus hygrometricus). These fungi are built with an inner puffball or spore sack surrounded by an outer rind. The rind splits to make a star, and the inner sack opens to release spores. The rays of the outer rind open and close depending on the humidity. The inner puffball is ephemeral, but the outer rinds are tough and last for years. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52603746859 I was looking under a piece of loose bark on a log in our woodpile today -- looking for slime molds or snails or whatever -- I found this tiny cricket! I believe it a Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae). This is small cricket, only about 3/16 inch long or less, not counting its antennae of course. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52603886855 This is a Woodlouse (Oniscidea, Isopoda, Crustacea) that cannot roll up into a ball. I call these ""sow bugs"" as distinct from the ""pill bugs"" that can roll into an into a ball. I found it under some bark in a woodpile. There are many different kinds, so I'll leave it at that. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52603676249 I'm pretty sure this is a transparent insect larva on a piece of oak bark in the soggy woods yesterday. Maybe it is the larva of a fly (Diptera)? It has an interesting ""mouth"". (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52605851044 I found this interesting spider under a loose piece of oak bark in our woodpile this morning. Maybe this spider is genus Kibramoa or Plectreurys in the spider family Plectreuridae? This one played dead when I found it, it even let me arrange its legs with a stick without complaining. I covered it up again with the bark after I got my photos. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2023)

occurrenceRemarks: 52607561146 This is a big black beetle trying to get away from me after I exposed its hiding place under a loose piece of oak bark in our woodpile yesterday. It might be a Darkling Beetle in the family Tenebrionidae, but it's smaller and faster than the larger Pinacate Beetles (genus Coelocnemis or Eleodes) that I sometimes find. Maybe it is the species Live Oak Cluster Beetle (Cibdelis blaschkei)? (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2023)

occurrenceRemarks: 52607964930 I'm pretty sure this is another Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider web with raindrops. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2022)

occurrenceRemarks: 52609991020 I found this handsome spider under a loose piece of oak bark in our woodpile this morning. I'm pretty sure this is a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2023)

It guess it is a Darkling Beetle in the family Tenebrionidae, maybe the species Live Oak Cluster Beetle (Cibdelis blaschkei). HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2023)

occurrenceRemarks: 52609795229 This is a tiny cricket showing its rear end. I believe it is a Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae). It was a under loose bark on a log in our woodpile a couple of days ago. This is small cricket, only about 3/16 inch long or less. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52612017835 I found these two different Ticks (Ixodidae, Arachnida) on dead stems of a perennial grass plant today -- probably Small-flowered Melic (Melica imperfecta, Poaceae) though it hardly matters. They are male and female Western Black-legged Ticks (Ixodes pacificus). Males lacks a scutum or shield that allows the female to swell up during its blood meal. The females need a blood meal to produce their eggs, but the males don't. These ticks are said to the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast, but apparently lizards protect us -- see this photo. Sounds fantastic, but apparently it's true. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52611564951 Here are pleurotoid mushrooms growing from Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) logs in our woodpile yesterday. Pleurotoid fungi all have a lateral stalk or no stalk at all, and take their name from the Oyster Mushroom (Pleurotus ostreatus). That's probably what these are, but maybe not -- there are other kinds. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52611977745 This is a ""just a fly"" (Diptera) in the woods a couple of days ago. My first guess based on its color and the way it holds its wings would be a Root-Maggot Fly in the family Anthomyiidae. Any help with identification will be appreciated. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
vernacularName: Chaparral Camel Cricket; prickly pear cactus
occurrenceRemarks: 52612987727 This is the rear end of a Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) in the flower scar on a ripening fruit of a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus. These crickets will become common -- and larger -- later in the spring, but they are just starting out. What's odd is that I've found them here before, see my photos here, here, here, here, and here. I don't know what the attraction is, but other crickets like it here as well -- see this photo of a different Tree Cricket on the fruit. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52613968713 I found these Blewit (Clitocybe nuda aka Lepista nuda) mushrooms growing in the mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods today. Fresh caps sometimes have real blue or violet colors, see this photo, but they soon fade to tan or brown like this one with just a hint of violet. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52613450301 This is Witch's Butter (Tremella mesenterica or kin), a familiar Jelly Fungus in the Tremelliales in the woods today. It's nice to see after months of drought. They are said to be edible and tasteless - no surprise, they're mostly water. These odd ""mushrooms"" appear after rain and then dry out into a hard yellow crust as it looses moisture. Did you notice the tiny Globular Springtail (Symphypleona, Collembola)? (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
scientificName: Agaricus albolutescens; A. moronii; Agaricus silvicola
occurrenceRemarks: 52616116064 Here are old and new brown-spored Agaricus mushrooms in deep mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. This mushroom has a sweet almond smell and tastes good in a nibble. I've been calling it the Amber-Staining Agaricus (Agaricus albolutescens aka A. moronii). Another possibility is Agaricus silvicola. I suspect they are edible and delicious, but I'm wary of all our woodland species of Agaricus. ""Once bitten, twice warned!"" (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2023)

vernacularName: Coast Live Oak; California Bay; Coastal Wood Fern; Wild Blackberry; Poison Oak; Nuttall's Snowberry
associatedTaxa: Dryopteris arguta; Umbellularia californica; Rubus ursinus; Toxicodendron diversilobum; Symphoricarpos mollis
occurrenceRemarks: 52616265490 Here is a photo of the little waterfall along our driveway -- it rarely flows, but here it is! It falls about 20 feet total. It's a small drainage and it takes some heavy rain to get it working. It's remarkable how quick it subsides when the rain slows down enough to get out with my camera. The woodland around the waterfall shows Coast Live Oak and California Bay trees, with an understory of Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta) and Wild Blackberry and still-dormant Poison Oak and Nuttall's Snowberry. I seem to get this shot whenever the waterfall is going, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52618649408 Here is another photo of the little waterfall along our driveway -- it rarely flows, but it was cranking in the rain today! We've had over a foot of rain since yesterday when this storm began. More is coming. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52617631867 I found this nice colony of pink-spored Entoloma mushrooms in the woods a couple of days ago. The gills start pallid, but in time they pick up the spore color like the upturned one. These don't have that chlorine bleach smell like a swimming pool that some of these mushrooms have, like this one, so I guess they fall into the ""Entoloma rhodopolium"" group -- maybe Entoloma lividoalbum, but who knows. These are growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees.

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52618402824 I believe these dark-spored mushrooms are part of the Psathyrella longipes group. They grow alone or in small groups on debris under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods. The dark spore-covered gills don't turn to ink like the genus Coprinus, and they don't grow on dung like the genus Panaeolus. The caps are ""hygrophanous"" -- changing color as they dry out. They are attractive mushrooms albeit very fragile. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52620023281 I believe this is the mushroom formally known as the Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila), now more properly called Gymnopus dryophilus -- though I suspect the common name will stick. You can tell by the fallen leaves that oak trees are nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52620443270 Here is a bank of new fronds of California Polypody (Polypodium californicum) fern in the Polypodiaceae plant family growing with moss on a sandstone outcrop today. My mom used to call these Resurrection Ferns because they seem to appear from nowhere when the rains arrive. They actually grow from a thick mass of rhizomes at the base of the fronds during our dry summer when they go dormant. These ferns are creatures of our shady woods in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52620437560 Here are tiny Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing on a piece of bark from Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. These look like little insect eggs, but something different will come from their spores, see this photo for a bit more info. I believe the photo shows sessile (without a stalk) sporangia or plasmodiocarps. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52622328290 Here are lots of tiny spiderlings on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I'm surprised they weren't washed away in the rain. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesday group, but Happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52621887286 This is a spider and webbing and under a piece of loose bark in our woodpile. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52622134419 This is webbing under a piece of loose bark in our woodpile a couple of days ago -- or is it fungus mycelium? Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52623741341 This is looking up into a new flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) in the woods today, first new flower I've noticed. I like the heart-shaped anthers, though I would actually call them sagittate (arrowhead-like) rather than cordate (heart-like). If you look close, you can see little pinhead glands on the ""petals"" (that are really sepals, the real petals are white). Leaves, flower stalks, and the flowers themselves are all glandular in this species. See this photo for a comparison of our different native gooseberries. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52624212483 Here are two shots of a tiny orb-weaving spider taken today, but it's a Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family instead of Araneidae. I believe it is species Metellina mimetoides by the shape of its abdomen. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52623209527 This is the rear end of a Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) under loose bark on a log in our woodpile a couple of days ago. These crickets will become common -- and larger -- later in the spring, but they are just starting out. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
vernacularName: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak
occurrenceRemarks: 52626044708 Here is a Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) on an Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) leaf in the woods today. They are gnarly-looking predators with that rough exterior and spike-like proboscis, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52625025852 These are Non-biting Midge Flies (Chironomidae) that dance in the sunbeams above the oak trees in the woods today, see this photo. A small swarm of the midges was flying right by the spider's web with the expected results. It's curious that most (all?) of the flies are male with feathery antennae, see this photo for comparison shots of male and female. That makes me wonder if all the flies in the swarms are male. See this photo for another web with lots of these tiny flies. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2023)These are Non-biting Midge Flies (Chironomidae) that dance in the sunbeams above the oak trees in the woods today, see this photo. accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
scientificName: Cortinarius collinitus; Cortinarius trivialis; Cortinarius glutinosoarmillatus
occurrenceRemarks: 52626010963 Here is a Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods today. More properly, it is part of the Cortinarius collinitus group which has been split into several variants. Maybe they are really Cortinarius trivialis or Cortinarius glutinosoarmillatus, but the old name is familiar and works for me. They are very slimy mushrooms. You can also see remnants of the ""cortina"" hairs on the stem below the cap, stained rusty-brown from falling spores -- see this photo for a closer look. That is typical for this genus. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52627660469 This is a small colony of ants on the inside of my camera bag yesterday. They weren't here the day before. I don't understand the different types of ants, but there are differences: there's a winged ant that could be male and a larger one that could be queen. They have found shelter from the rain. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52627877013 This cute little spider is a Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on the windshield on the little electric cart I use to get around in our canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
vernacularName: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak
occurrenceRemarks: 52627866138 Here is another shot a Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) on an Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) leaf in the woods yesterday, see this photo. They are gnarly-looking predators with that rough exterior and spike-like proboscis, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52629681606 This is a tiny Barklouse (Psocoptera aka Psocodea) in our the rain gauge today! It's a more complicated rain gauge than the coffee can I used to use, as it has a small inner tube which measures hundredths of an inch and overflows into a larger outer tube which can hold up to ten inches. There's a funnel-like lid on top that directs water into the small tube. The critter seems to want to be inside and made no effort to get away when I took the lid off today. Compare this photo, this photo, and this photo of some other Barklice I've found. I don't notice these very often, though I suspect the woods are full of them. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52629665281 Here are the first new flowers of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family in the woods today. The tree is related to sassafras and avocado and has similar surprisingly intricate flowers, see this photo for a closer look at a single flower. Sure, you can use the leaves as ""bay leaves"" for cooking -- but don't use too much, it's strong stuff! (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
vernacularName: Rufous Candy Cap; Coast Live Oak
occurrenceRemarks: 52629120392 Maybe these are Rufous Candy Cap (Lactarius rufulus) mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods today. I sliced the gills with my knife, and they have latex that is white changing to yellow. They don't have the maple-syrup smell of the real Candy Cap (Lactarius fragilis). (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52632312968 These are lovely little Pyxie Cup Lichens (genus Cladonia) growing on sandstone in the woods. They are a fruticose lichen with a stalk, but they come from a foliose base. There are several different kinds, so I won't try to guess which one this is. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52632252600 I found this Blewit (Clitocybe nuda aka Lepista nuda) mushroom growing in the mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods yesterday. Fresh caps sometimes have real blue or violet colors like this, but they soon fade to tan or brown with just a hint of violet, compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52632054459 I found this Cortinarius mushroom pushing up through deep mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees after our good rains a few days ago. Like all Cortinarius mushrooms, this one has rusty-colored spores which stain everything - especially the shaggy hairs on its stem that originally form a veil to protect the gills. The problem is they end up looking alike, so there's no good way to tell them apart -- but they are handsome mushrooms when they are fresh like this. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52634302455 This is another small Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider in native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. I believe this is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) with that ""folium pattern"" on its abdomen, see this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52633844761 I found another Cortinarius mushroom pushing up through deep mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees after our recent good rains today. Like all Cortinarius mushrooms, this one has rusty-colored spores. The problem is they end up looking alike, so there's no good way to tell them apart -- but they are handsome mushrooms when they are fresh like this. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52634275585 These are the tunnels of leaf-miner caterpillars meandering through a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods a few days ago. You can follow the path of the caterpillars' life from a small start to a big finish, growing as they move along. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
vernacularName: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
occurrenceRemarks: 52636043274 Here is a mushroom-like spring gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis, Cynipidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). These are ""bisexual galls"" because the will give rise to both male and female wasps that will mate and produce the summer unisexual ""oak apple"" galls, see this photo, which contain only female wasps. Those ladies will emerge next year and produce another crop of bisexual galls like the one in my photo, and the cycle repeats. Sorry, but galls are complicated like that. It's part of what makes then so interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52636252883 Spiders aren't the only ones! This is a caterpillar and webbing (and raindrops) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52636201210 This is a webbing under a piece of loose bark in our woodpile. I have another photo of the spider in this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52637064627 I believe this is a Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) hiding under a leaf Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52637056577 I found this small spider under a loose piece of oak bark in our woodpile this morning. I'm pretty sure this is a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider, maybe the same kind as this one. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52637805179 These are different critters under a piece of oak bark in our woodpile. I believe there is a Jumping Bristletail (Machilidae) on the left and a Woodlouse (Isopoda) on the right. The woodlouse is showing its rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52639203446 This is a Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) on old stalk of a Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. It's tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52639667593 This is a golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a old leaf of Chaparral Yucca (Hesperoyucca whipplei, Agavaceae) in the woods today. This fly might be species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California, though it might be something else like Eupeodes fumipennis. Whatever it is, it's a handsome fly. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52639660118 This is a Non-biting Midge Fly (Chironomidae) that usually dance in the sunbeams above the oak trees in the woods a couple of days ago, compare this photo. This one is male with feathery antennae, see this photo for comparison shots of male and female. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52640539687 I found this cute little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) looking at its shadow on a fallen oak leaf today. I believe it is genus Bombylius, probably the Greater Bee Fly (Bombylius major) which is said to appear early. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2023)

occurrenceRemarks: 52641294199 This is another shot of a small Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider in native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today, see this photo. I believe this is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) with that ""folium pattern"" on its abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52641027931 I found this small Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris, Oxyopidae) on an old leaf of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods yesterday. Its heavily spined legs give it away as a Lynx Spider, related to the more familiar Green Lynx Spider in this photo, but this one is much smaller. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2023)

occurrenceRemarks: 52643687980 Here are tiny spiders on stems of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) that's gone to seed in the woods today. I believe they are Mesh Web Weaver spiders in the family Dictynidae, maybe genus Dictyna itself -- at least it matches the illustration for that genus in my book. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52642702447 I found these mysterious orbs in leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday. Maybe they are eggs or pupal cases? They look like are wrapped in silk. It reminds me of craft projects I've seen with string or paper mache wrapped around a balloon with glue, then the balloon is popped after the glue dries - like this. I wonder how it was made without a balloon? Is there something in here?? I've found similar ones in the woods, see this photo and this photo. I love finding these little mysteries. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52643180811 This is the tunnel of a tiny leaf-miner on a leaf of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in the woods yesterday. I suppose it's another leaf-miner caterpillar (or several?), but compare this one and this one -- it appears to have many branches. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52645820245 This is of our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae in the canyon today. These dragonflies stay active in the canyon all winter long, though they sit out cold and stormy days by finding an out-of-the-way perch. They keep moving around, so it's always a surprise when you suddenly notice one right in front of you. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52645622324 This is another small Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider in the woods today, but this one is on a different plant -- native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) that's gone to seed, see this photo. I believe this is another Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) with that ""folium pattern"" on its abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52645796545 Here are new flowers of (I believe) our native dry-land Round-fruited Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family growing in our canyon today. You can see the shaggy male staminate flowers on the left, and the female pistillate flowers are already developing into seeds lower down on the stems. It's a remarkably tough grass-like plant that grows in the shaded woods, but also in glaring sun. It's not at all like a typical wet-land sedge, except for the flowers. I really admire the toughness of this plant. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52647407831 Here is another Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly among fallen oak leaves in the woods today. This is one of a group of large butterflies that overwinter as adults and come out on warm days -- including the Mourning Cloak and California Tortoiseshell. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52647870343 I found this mysterious orb in a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods, see this photo. Now I believe it is the pupa of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae, Neuroptera) insect, see this photo at BugGuide. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52647857498 This is a liverwort (Marchantiophyta) on a sandstone outcrop in our canyon, but I believe it is different from the usual California Liverwort (Asterella californica). For one thing, it's growing in a sunnier place. The sporophytes are taller and smoother and less divided, and the creeping ""leaves"" (""thallus"") are smaller, compare this photo. This one might be Palmer's Asterella (Asterella palmeri), see the photos at CalPhotos. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52649750943 Here is a Green Lacewing (family Chrysopidae, Neuroptera) on the chair that I use in the woods. Those eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52649259746 Spiders aren't the only ones! This is webbing on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), and the frass (poop) shows that it's the work of a caterpillar rather than a spider. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2023)

I suspect this is the web of a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae, it's near the spider in this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2023)

occurrenceRemarks: 52651603793 I believe this is a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera) on a mossy rock in the woods today. It is uncommonly handsome! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2023)

(San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2023)

occurrenceRemarks: 52655336169 I found this skeletonized leaf on a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This may be the work of a caterpillar like the one in this photo, but fungi also do this. I'm not sure how to tell the actions apart when a leaf gets to this stage, but they are beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2023)

occurrenceRemarks: 52655491415 I believe this is a male Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes, Linyphiidae) hiding under a leaf Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2023)

occurrenceRemarks: 52655487605 This is looking into the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider in the woods yesterday. I've tried to photograph this spider several times, but whenever I get too close it zips away faster than I can see. This time I was ready! (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52657754748 I found this interesting spider under a loose piece of oak bark in our woodpile this morning. Maybe this spider is genus Kibramoa in the spider family Plectreuridae -- and maybe Kibramoa madrona)? This one played dead when I found it, and I covered it up again with the bark after I got my photos. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52657249901 Here are new flowers of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family in the woods today. The tree is related to sassafras and avocado and has similar surprisingly intricate flowers, see this photo for a closer look at a single flower. Sure, you can use the leaves as ""bay leaves"" for cooking -- but don't use too much, it's strong stuff! (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52657736673 Here is a bank of new fronds of California Polypody (Polypodium californicum) fern in the Polypodiaceae plant family growing with moss on a sandstone outcrop today. My mom used to call these Resurrection Ferns because they seem to appear from nowhere when the rains arrive. They actually grow from a thick mass of rhizomes at the base of the fronds during our dry summer when they go dormant. These ferns are creatures of our shady woods in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52659180992 This is a new flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) in the woods today. I got the photo in light rain today that was just right to leave a few raindrops. If you look close, you can see little pinhead glands on the ""petals"" (that are really sepals, the real petals are white). Leaves, flower stalks, and the flowers themselves are all glandular in this species. See this photo for a comparison of our different native gooseberries. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52660079710 This is a new plant of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. This will become the violet form of these plants, see this photo for the lavender flowers. See this photo and this photo of other forms. These plants seem to be true biennials which form leaves in their first year, then flower in their second year. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52659132362 This is a bank of liverworts (Marchantiophyta) growing on sandstone in the woods today. I believe these are the California Liverwort (Asterella californica). These liverworts fade to black as they dry out and recover in a day with some rain, compare this photo. In time they will develop little sexual sporophytes, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52661208182 Here is a striking fly (Diptera) in the woods today that looks for all the world like a bee. I believe this is a Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera), possibly a Narcissus Bulb Fly (Merodon equestris) or kin in the same Eristalini tribe that includes many more bee-mimic flies in the genus Eristalis. It's furry like a bee and even has flattened leg segments like a bee. It's my understanding that this fly has been introduced from Europe and is now widespread in North America. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52661695971 These are new leaves of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) in woods today. These shrubs are drought-deciduous, and the plants looked quite dead a month ago. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2023)

accessRights: Use permitted under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0)
occurrenceRemarks: 52662122640 Here are many galled flowers of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). That odd beaked shape is typical. I'm pretty sure these are the work of a Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera), see this photo of an emerging adult. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2023)

52665244637 This is a tiny spider on a cactus spine in the garden this morning. It is a well-endowed male, maybe a Dwarf Spider (Erigoninae) in the Linyphiidae? (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2023)

Today is Groundhog Day, midway between solstice and equinox, and we are getting more light in our winter-shady canyon. any groundhog or gopher that stuck its head out today did not see its shadow - portending early spring?

These crickets will become common -- and larger -- later in the spring. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2023)
